questions.question:
Which of the following statements is true?
A. SLR parser is more powerful than LALR
B. LALR parser is more powerful than Canonical LR parser
C. Canonical LR parser is more powerful than LALR parser
D. The parsers SLR, Canonical LR, and LALR have the same power
questions.answer: C. Canonical LR parser is more powerful than LALR parser
questions.to_find: Which statement correctly describes the relative power of SLR, LALR, and Canonical LR parsers

understand.plain: There are three bottom-up LR parsing techniques: SLR(1), LALR(1), and Canonical LR(1). They differ in how they resolve shift-reduce conflicts. More powerful means the parser can handle a larger class of grammars without conflicts. The question asks which relationship among these three parser types is correct.

keywords.explain: Simple LR parser that uses FOLLOW sets to decide lookahead. It has fewer states but is least powerful — it may have conflicts where LALR/CLR do not.
keywords.example: A grammar that causes a shift/reduce conflict in SLR due to a broad FOLLOW set but not in LALR.

keywords.explain: Look-Ahead LR parser that merges states in the Canonical LR automaton that have the same core items. It is more powerful than SLR but less powerful than Canonical LR.
keywords.example: An LALR parser can handle many grammars that SLR cannot, but may still have conflicts for some grammars that CLR handles without conflict.

keywords.explain: The most powerful LR(1) parser — it uses the complete set of LR(1) items. It never has conflicts for any LALR grammar, and can also handle some grammars that LALR cannot.
keywords.example: CLR(1) has separate states for items with different lookaheads, preventing conflicts that arise from merging in LALR.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='140' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='80' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>SLR(1)</text><text x='80' y='75' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Least powerful</text><text x='80' y='95' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Uses FOLLOW</text><text x='80' y='115' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Fewest states</text><rect x='190' y='20' width='140' height='120' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>LALR(1)</text><text x='260' y='75' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Middle power</text><text x='260' y='95' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Merges CLR states</text><text x='260' y='115' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Same #states as SLR</text><rect x='370' y='20' width='140' height='120' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='440' y='50' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>CLR(1)</text><text x='440' y='75' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Most powerful</text><text x='440' y='95' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Full lookahead</text><text x='440' y='115' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>Most states</text><text x='155' y='85' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'><</text><text x='335' y='85' class='big ink' text-anchor='middle'><</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three boxes showing SLR(1) < LALR(1) < CLR(1) in order of parser power, left to right.

given.aim: Determine the correct relationship among SLR, LALR, and Canonical LR parsers in terms of expressive power.

terms.meaning: The class of grammars the parser can handle without conflicts. A more powerful parser handles a strictly larger set of grammars.
terms.example: CLR(1) handles every LALR grammar plus some extras.
terms.connects: Defines the partial order: SLR subset LALR subset CLR.

terms.meaning: SLR uses the FOLLOW set of a non-terminal as lookahead for reduce actions, which is coarser than LALR's item-specific lookaheads.
terms.example: If FOLLOW(A) = {a, b} but a specific item only needs {a}, SLR may still allow reducing on b causing a conflict.
terms.connects: Makes SLR less powerful because it over-approximates lookaheads.

terms.meaning: LALR merges CLR states with identical cores but different lookaheads. This merging can introduce reduce-reduce conflicts that CLR avoids.
terms.example: Two CLR states with the same core items but disjoint lookaheads get merged in LALR, combining their lookaheads and potentially causing a conflict.
terms.connects: Makes CLR strictly more powerful than LALR.

terms.meaning: SLR and LALR have the same number of states (formed from LR(0) core items). CLR generally has more states because it keeps states with different lookaheads separate.
terms.example: A grammar with 10 LALR states might have 15 CLR states.
terms.connects: More states in CLR = finer discrimination = fewer conflicts = more power.

given.plan: Recall the hierarchy of LR parsers: every grammar parseable by SLR is parseable by LALR, and every grammar parseable by LALR is parseable by CLR. The inclusions are strict, so CLR is strictly more powerful than LALR, which is strictly more powerful than SLR.

steps.title: Recall the power hierarchy
steps.formula_raw: SLR(1) subset LALR(1) subset CLR(1) (all strict subsets)
steps.apply: Every SLR(1) grammar is LALR(1), and every LALR(1) grammar is CLR(1), but not vice versa. This means CLR(1) > LALR(1) > SLR(1) in expressive power.
steps.note: SLR, LALR and CLR all have the same number of parser states (same LR(0) core for SLR/LALR; CLR has more states). The difference is in how conflicts are resolved, not how many states there are for SLR vs LALR.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply: A: SLR > LALR — FALSE (SLR is weaker). B: LALR > CLR — FALSE (LALR is weaker). C: CLR > LALR — TRUE. D: All equal — FALSE (strict inclusions exist).
steps.note: Option C is the correct statement.

solution.result: C. Canonical LR parser is more powerful than LALR parser

questions.formula_note: LR parser power hierarchy: SLR(1) subset LALR(1) subset CLR(1), all strict inclusions.

questions.question:
Assume that the SLR parser for a grammar G has n_1 states and the LALR parser for G has n_2 states. The relationship between n_1 and n_2 is:
A. n_1 is necessarily less than n_2
B. n_1 is necessarily equal to n_2
C. n_1 is necessarily greater than n_2
D. None of the above
questions.answer: B. n_1 is necessarily equal to n_2
questions.to_find: The relationship between the number of states in the SLR parser and the LALR parser for the same grammar G

understand.plain: Both SLR(1) and LALR(1) parsers are built from the same set of LR(0) items. The states in these two parsers correspond to the same sets of LR(0) items. The only difference between them is in the lookahead sets used for reduce actions — SLR uses FOLLOW sets and LALR uses more precise computed lookaheads. The number of states does not change.

keywords.explain: An LR(0) item is a production rule with a dot (.) marking how far parsing has progressed. The states of both SLR and LALR parsers are sets of LR(0) items.
keywords.example: For A -> BC, items are: A -> .BC, A -> B.C, A -> BC.

keywords.explain: Each state in an SLR or LALR parser is a set of LR(0) items. Since both use the same LR(0) automaton structure, they have the same number of states.
keywords.example: A grammar that generates 8 LR(0) states will have 8 states in both SLR and LALR parsers.

understand.visual_alt: SLR and LALR parsers are both built on the same LR(0) canonical collection of item sets, so their number of states n_1 and n_2 are always equal.

given.aim: Find the relationship between the number of parser states in SLR(1) and LALR(1) parsers for the same grammar.

terms.meaning: The number of states in the SLR(1) parse table for grammar G.
terms.example: If G has 10 canonical LR(0) item sets, SLR has 10 states.
terms.connects: Determined entirely by the LR(0) canonical collection of G.

terms.meaning: The number of states in the LALR(1) parse table for grammar G.
terms.example: LALR is built by starting with LR(1) items and merging states with the same LR(0) core. The resulting count equals the number of LR(0) states.
terms.connects: After merging, LALR has exactly as many states as the LR(0) automaton, which is the same as SLR.

terms.meaning: SLR states = canonical LR(0) item sets. LALR states = merged LR(1) item sets (merged by core). Both methods yield the same number of states.
terms.example: If two LR(1) items differ only in lookahead (same core), they are merged into one LALR state, recovering the LR(0) count.
terms.connects: This is why n_1 = n_2 always.

given.plan: Recall that SLR builds states directly from LR(0) items. LALR builds states from LR(1) items but then merges any two states that have the same LR(0) core. After merging, the number of states in LALR equals the number of LR(0) item sets, which is exactly the same as SLR.

steps.title: Understand how SLR and LALR states are constructed
steps.apply: SLR parser states are exactly the sets in the canonical LR(0) collection of G. LALR parser is constructed by: (1) building the canonical LR(1) collection, then (2) merging every pair of states that share the same LR(0) core (differ only in lookaheads). After merging, each merged state corresponds to exactly one LR(0) state.
steps.note: The merging step in LALR construction is specifically designed to recover the LR(0) state count.

steps.title: Conclude the relationship
steps.apply: Since the number of LALR states after merging = number of distinct LR(0) cores = number of SLR states, we have n_1 = n_2 for any grammar G.
steps.note: Note: CLR(1) generally has n_3 >= n_2 states since it does not merge any states.

solution.result: B. n_1 is necessarily equal to n_2

questions.formula_note: SLR and LALR parsers are both built on the canonical LR(0) item sets; LALR is obtained by merging LR(1) states with the same core, yielding the same state count as SLR.

questions.question:
Consider the grammar:
S -> (S) | a
Let the number of states in SLR(1), LR(1) and LALR(1) parsers for the grammar be n_1, n_2 and n_3 respectively. The following relationship holds good:
A. n_1 < n_2 and n_3 = n_2
B. n_1 = n_3 and n_3 < n_2
C. n_1 = n_3 = n_2
D. n_1 < n_3 and n_3 = n_2
questions.answer: B. n_1 = n_3 and n_3 < n_2
questions.to_find: Relationship between the number of states in SLR(1), LR(1), and LALR(1) parsers for the grammar S -> (S) | a

understand.plain: We need to build or estimate the number of parser states for a simple grammar in SLR(1), LR(1), and LALR(1). The key insight is: SLR and LALR always have the same number of states (both derived from LR(0) item sets), while LR(1) may have more because it distinguishes states with different lookaheads separately.

keywords.explain: An LR(1) item is an LR(0) item paired with a lookahead symbol. States are sets of LR(1) items. Two states with the same LR(0) items but different lookaheads are kept separate in LR(1) but merged in LALR.
keywords.example: [A -> alpha.beta, a] and [A -> alpha.beta, b] are different LR(1) items for the same LR(0) item A -> alpha.beta.

keywords.explain: LALR merges pairs of LR(1) states that have identical LR(0) cores (differ only in lookaheads). This reduces state count from LR(1) down to the LR(0) count.
keywords.example: If LR(1) has 20 states and 5 pairs share LR(0) cores, LALR has 20 - 5 = 15 states = LR(0) count.

understand.visual_alt: For grammar S -> (S)|a: SLR(1) has n_1 states based on LR(0) items, LR(1) has n_2 states using full lookahead items, LALR(1) has n_3 states after merging LR(1) states. n_1 = n_3 < n_2.

given.aim: Count or compare the number of parser states for SLR(1), LR(1), and LALR(1) for the grammar S -> (S) | a.

terms.meaning: A simple context-free grammar generating strings like a, (a), ((a)), etc.
terms.connects: This grammar is unambiguous and is parseable by all three parser types. The state counts can be compared concretely.

terms.meaning: States in the SLR(1) parser = canonical LR(0) item sets for this grammar.
terms.example: Augmented grammar: S' -> S. The LR(0) automaton has a specific number of states.
terms.connects: n_1 = number of distinct LR(0) item sets.

terms.meaning: States in the Canonical LR(1) parser. Each state is a set of LR(1) items (LR(0) item + lookahead). States with the same LR(0) core but different lookaheads are separate.
terms.example: For this grammar, some LR(0) states split into two LR(1) states with different lookaheads.
terms.connects: n_2 >= n_1 always, often strictly greater.

terms.meaning: States in LALR(1) = LR(1) states after merging those with the same LR(0) core.
terms.example: After merging, the count comes back to the number of distinct LR(0) cores, which equals n_1.

given.plan: Use the fundamental facts: n_1 = n_3 (SLR and LALR have the same number of states, both equal to the number of canonical LR(0) item sets), and n_2 >= n_3 (LR(1) has at least as many states as LALR). For most non-trivial grammars n_2 > n_3. Construct the LR(0) automaton for this grammar to verify n_1.

steps.title: Construct the augmented grammar and LR(0) items
steps.apply:
Augmented: S' -> S. Productions: (1) S' -> S, (2) S -> (S), (3) S -> a.
LR(0) item sets:
I0: S' -> .S, S -> .(S), S -> .a
I1: S' -> S. [on S]
I2: S -> (.S), S -> .(S), S -> .a [on '(']
I3: S -> a. [on 'a']
I4: S -> (S.), S -> .(S), S -> .a [on S from I2]
I5: S -> (S). [on ')' from I4]

From I4, on '(': goto I2 (already exists); on 'a': goto I3 (already exists).
Total LR(0) states: I0, I1, I2, I3, I4, I5 = 6 states. So n_1 = 6.
steps.note: The goto on '(' from I2 and I4 both go to I2; goto on 'a' from I2 and I4 both go to I3. This is fine for LR(0).

steps.title: Count LR(1) states
steps.apply:
For LR(1), each LR(0) state may split into multiple states with different lookaheads. For this grammar:
- I0: S' -> .S, $ ; S -> .(S), $ ; S -> .a, $
- I1: S' -> S., $
- I2_outer: S -> (.S), $ ; S -> .(S), )/$ ; S -> .a, )/$
- I2_inner: S -> (.S), ) ; S -> .(S), ) ; S -> .a, ) (reached from I4)
  Actually I2 in LR(0) splits: when reached from I0 the lookahead for S -> .a is $, when reached from I4 (inside nested parens) lookahead is ')'.
  So I2 in LR(0) becomes two LR(1) states: I2a (from I0, lookahead $) and I2b (from I4, lookahead ')). Similarly I3 and I4 may split.
- This gives n_2 > 6, i.e., n_2 > n_1.

After merging the split LR(1) states (LALR), we recover n_3 = n_1 = 6.
steps.note: The exact count of n_2 for this grammar is typically 9 or 10 LR(1) states, but the key relationship is n_1 = n_3 < n_2.

steps.title: Determine the relationship
steps.apply: n_1 = 6 (LR(0)/SLR states), n_3 = 6 (LALR states after merging = LR(0) count), n_2 > 6 (LR(1) keeps split states separate). So n_1 = n_3 and n_3 < n_2, which is option B.
steps.note: This relationship n_1 = n_3 <= n_2 holds for any grammar; strict inequality n_3 < n_2 holds when the grammar forces some LR(0) states to split in LR(1).

solution.result: B. n_1 = n_3 and n_3 < n_2

questions.formula_note: For any grammar: #SLR states = #LALR states (both equal canonical LR(0) item-set count); #LR(1) states >= #LALR states, with strict inequality when some LR(0) states split under different lookaheads.

questions.question:
Consider the following grammar:
S -> S + E
E -> E * F
E -> F
F -> id

Consider the following LR(0) items corresponding to the grammar above:
i.  E -> E * .F
ii. E -> E . * F
iii. F -> .id

Given the items above, which two of them will appear in the same set in the canonical sets-of-items for the grammar?
A. i and ii
B. i and iii
C. ii and iii
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Which two of the given LR(0) items appear together in the same canonical item set

understand.plain: In LR parsing, a canonical item set (a parser state) is a closed set of LR(0) items. When we have an item with a dot before a non-terminal (like E -> E * .F), we add all productions for that non-terminal with the dot at the beginning (closure). The question asks which of the three items share a state in the LR(0) automaton.

keywords.explain: A production rule with a dot (.) showing the parser's current position. Everything before the dot has been seen; everything after must still be matched.
keywords.example: E -> E . * F means the parser has matched E and expects * next.

keywords.explain: If a set contains item [A -> alpha . B beta], then for every production B -> gamma, the item [B -> . gamma] is also added. This is the closure operation.
keywords.example: Closure({E -> E * .F}) adds F -> .id because F appears after the dot in E -> E*.F.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 460 200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='440' height='170' rx='10' class='hi'/><text x='230' y='40' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>One LR(0) Item Set (State)</text><text x='50' y='70' class='sub ink'>i.  E -> E * .F</text><text x='50' y='100' class='sub mut'>(dot before F; closure adds F productions)</text><text x='50' y='130' class='sub ink'>iii. F -> .id</text><text x='50' y='155' class='sub mut'>(added by closure of item i)</text><rect x='20' y='58' width='420' height='108' rx='6' class='bg'/><text x='40' y='85' class='sub ink'>Closure: E -> E * .F  =>  add F -> .id</text><text x='40' y='110' class='sub ink'>So items i and iii are in the SAME state</text><text x='40' y='140' class='sub mut'>Item ii (E -> E . * F) is in a different state</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Item i (E -> E*.F) is in an item set that also contains F -> .id (item iii) by closure. Item ii (E -> E.*F) is in a different state.

given.aim: Determine which two of the three given LR(0) items appear in the same canonical item set.

terms.meaning: The parser has matched 'E *' and now expects to match F. The dot is before the non-terminal F.
terms.example: This item appears in the state reached after shifting '*' from the state containing E -> E . * F.
terms.connects: Because dot is before F, closure adds all F-productions with dot at the start.

terms.meaning: The parser has matched 'E' and now expects to see '*'. The dot is before the terminal '*'.
terms.example: This item appears in whatever state is reached after matching E in the context where E -> E * F is being parsed.
terms.connects: This is a different state from item i; it is the predecessor state before shifting '*'.

terms.meaning: The parser is about to match 'id' to complete production F -> id. Dot is at the very beginning.
terms.example: This item is generated by closure whenever any item has a dot before F (like item i).
terms.connects: It gets added to the same state as item i by the closure operation.

given.plan: Apply the closure operation to item i (E -> E * .F). Since the dot precedes non-terminal F, add all productions for F with dot at start. F has one production: F -> id, so add F -> .id (item iii). Thus items i and iii are in the same item set. Item ii has the dot before '*' (a terminal), so it's a different state — the predecessor state before shifting '*'.

steps.title: Identify the state containing item i
steps.apply:
Start with item i: E -> E * .F. The dot is before non-terminal F. Apply closure: for each production F -> gamma, add F -> .gamma.
F -> id is the only F-production, so add: F -> .id (this is item iii).
The closure of {E -> E * .F} = {E -> E * .F, F -> .id} = {item i, item iii}.
steps.note: The closure of a set containing E -> E * .F must include F -> .id. So items i and iii are always in the same state.

steps.title: Verify item ii is in a different state
steps.apply: Item ii is E -> E . * F. The dot is before the terminal '*', not before any non-terminal, so no new items are added by closure for this item. This state is a different state (the one before the '*' transition) — specifically the state that transitions to the state containing item i on reading '*'. So item ii is in a different state from items i and iii.
steps.note: Items i and ii cannot be in the same set because item i is reached by going to a new state on '*', while item ii is in the source state.

steps.title: Identify the correct answer
steps.apply: Items i and iii are in the same canonical item set (by closure). Items ii is in a different set. Answer is B: i and iii.
steps.note: This is a direct application of the closure operation in LR(0) item set construction.

questions.formula_note: LR(0) closure: if [A -> alpha.B beta] is in a state, then [B -> .gamma] is also in that state for every B-production. This places items i and iii (E -> E*.F and F -> .id) in the same canonical item set.
